items.title: As US forces struck targets in Iran again, President Trump hints there's more to come | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US forces struck targets across Iran overnight, marking thirteen consecutive days of attacks. President Donald Trump is hinting there's more to come, telling a US media outlet the Iranians 'havent received enough pain yet.' Iran says it struck US assets in the Gulf region in response. And UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres is warning against escalations, saying the region is 'teetering on the edge of the unimaginable.'

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: More than 80,000 forced to evacuate as fires rage across France and Spain | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Spain has declared a national emergency, as wildfires forced the removal to safety of tens of thousands of people near Madrid. A summer of extreme heat and dryness means the country is struggling to tackle several severe blazes. Fires are also burning out of control in France.  DW News correspondent Nicole Ris reports from outside Madrid and we speak to journalist Diane de Vignemont in Paris.

00:00 Spain declares national emergency over wildfires
00:21 Wildfires burn out of control in France

items.title: International Criminal Court member states remove prosecutor Karim Khan by majority vote | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Member states of the International Criminal Court voted to dismiss the court's chief prosecutor Karim Khan who faces allegations of sexually harassing a junior colleague. Khan denies the allegations, claiming that they're part of a campaign against him - and the court - following his investigation of Israeli officials of Israel's actions in Gaza. His lawyer says Khan will challenge his dismissal.

items.title: India's 'Cockroach' movement: Accusations of police brutality in New Delhi | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>There have been accusations of police brutality in New Delhi after police used tear gas and batons to stop thousands of anti-government protesters marching towards Parliament. The clashes left around 60 people injured. Protesters from India's Cockroach Janta Party accuse police of using excessive force and carrying out arbitrary detentions. The movement is demanding the resignation of India’s education minister over the alleged leak of medical school entrance tests. DW's Adil Bhat reports. 

00:00 India’s “Cockroach” movement alleges police brutality
00:36 Protester and family father is detained by police
01:17 Injured demonstrators returns to protests
04:18 Trust in authorities is shattered
04:46 DW's Adil Bhat reports from Delhi  
05:02 The mood among protesters is rather tense
05:34 People gather in large numbers, chant slogans, demand the resignation of the education minister
06:28 The outcome of a meeting between student protesters and the government
07:30 The government wants to buy time
08:00 The protest movement is not tied to any ideology, it will continue even if there is no leadership

items.title: What does forced labor really have to do with Trump's tariffs? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Are the new US tariffs really intended to combat forced labor around the globe, as Washington claims? Or is it about the Trump administration asserting its power?

The United States has slapped new tariffs on 60 economies. After facing legal challenges to previous tariffs, the Trump administration is making use of a little-known law that allows it to impose levies on countries that buy products made by forced labor.

Simon J. Evenett, professor at IMD Business School, tells host Pablo Foley Elias that the forced labor explanation is a "contrivance." DW's Kassandra Sundt explains that the tariffs place a high burden on vulnerable countries to prove that they do not buy products made by forced labor.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Why Brent crude is back up at $100 a barrel and how it could impact you | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Oil is back above $100 a barrel for the first time since May, and the situation in the Middle East is getting even more complicated. With Iran-backed Houthis targeting shipping  in the Red Sea and critical oil routes coming under pressure, what happens next for global energy markets? Ben Cahill of the Atlantic Council breaks down what's driving prices higher, who stands to lose the most, and whether consumers should be preparing for another inflationary shock.  

Chapters:
00:00 Intro
01:22 Saudi oil exports at risk
02:59 Houthi attacks in the Red Sea
04:33 Growing threat to global supply
08:00 How high could oil prices go?
10:40 Rising inflation and energy costs
13:34 EVs, fuel bills and consumer impact

#oilprices #usa #iranwar #houthis #dwnews
